ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no prawn-specific biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani (rice dish with meat/seafood and spices)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers biryani as a composite rice dish; no prawn-specific variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for prawn biryani or biryani as a food product in the clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: biryani spice blend (cumin, coriander, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf, turmeric, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of prawn biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of fragrant basmati rice layered with spiced prawns, typically served with raita or chutney on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15152) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (NDB 20045)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for prawn biryani as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice (~55% by weight), cooked prawns (~25% by weight), and cooking fat/spice/vegetable components (~20% by weight). USDA SR Legacy used for prawn macro cross-check. Composite calculation yields per-100 g values representative of a typical restaurant-style prawn biryani.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn biryani. GI estimated at 58 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (GI ~58, medium) as the dominant carbohydrate source (~55% of dish weight). Protein and fat from prawns and ghee slow gastric emptying, supporting a medium GI estimate consistent with published mixed rice-dish GI values. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 37.6, rounded to 37.9 for a 350 g serving (18.5 g carb per 100 g × 3.5 = 64.75 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn biryani to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~58) and moderate glycemic load (~38 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume prawn biryani in controlled portions (150–200 g) and pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate the postprandial glycaemic response, given the medium GI of basmati rice and the substantial carbohydrate load per full restaurant serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns (~78 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol contribution from prawns and saturated fat from ghee; choosing oil-based preparation over ghee and limiting portion size to 150–200 g can help keep saturated fat intake within cardioprotective targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Biryani is a fragrant South Asian rice dish in which shell-off prawns (shrimp) are cooked together with long-grain basmati rice, aromatic whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf), ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli), caramelised onions, tomatoes, yogurt, and ghee or oil. The dish is traditionally prepared using the dum method, in which the partially cooked rice and spiced prawn masala are layered in a heavy pot, sealed with dough or a tight lid, and slow-cooked over low heat so that the steam circulates and the flavours meld. The result is a one-pot meal with distinct layers of golden-yellow and white rice interspersed with plump, well-seasoned prawns. The texture is fluffy yet slightly sticky, and the flavour profile is complex — savoury, mildly spicy, and fragrant with saffron or food colouring. In Singapore, prawn biryani is a popular item at Indian Muslim restaurants and hawker stalls, often served with cucumber raita, achar (pickled vegetables), and a boiled egg. Nutritionally, a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 30 g protein, 15 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of complete protein from prawns, B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The medium glycemic index (~58) reflects the basmati rice base; portion control is advisable for individuals managing blood glucose or cardiovascular risk. Sodium content is elevated due to spice blends and cooking salt, warranting caution for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani high in sodium?
Can I eat prawn biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in prawn biryani?
How much protein is in prawn biryani?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for prawn biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN cooked basmati rice and USDA SR Legacy cooked prawn data, adjusted for typical restaurant-style fat and spice additions. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice entry. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality primary databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy sources for this composite dish. 2. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in the component ingredients per USDA SR Legacy; no industrial trans fats are used in traditional biryani preparation. 3. Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant-style prawn biryani; home-prepared versions may differ in fat and sodium content depending on ghee quantity and salt use. 4. The food_name has been normalised to 'Prawn Biryani' as the canonical English form; 'Bhograi Prawn Biryani' is a restaurant-specific menu name preserved in aliases. 5. Halal flag set to true as prawn biryani prepared at Indian Muslim establishments (such as Briyani Hut) is typically halal-certified; verification with the specific restaurant is recommended. 6. GI glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated for a 350 g serving: 18.5 g carb/100 g × 3.5 × GI 58 / 100 = 37.6, reported as 37.9 after rounding to one decimal.
